title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN EXODUS Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to give an overview of the story of Moses leading the children of Israel from Egypt into the promised land.
weekTheme: Boys get frustrated, Men have patience Learning to make the shift from self to others is the hardest thing about growing up. It’s a shift most men never fully make. The main reason is that loving people is really hard. Fyodor Dostoevsky, a really cool Russian author wrote: “Love in action is a harsh and dreadful thing compared to love in dreams. Love in dreams is greedy for immediate action, rapidly performed and in the sight of all. Men will even give their lives if only the ordeal does not last long but is soon over, with all looking on and applauding as though on stage. But active love is labor and fortitude…” It's easy to love for a minute when it feels good, it doesn’t cost or inconvenience you, and everyone around you is cheering for you. But real love is long, slow, and patient. No one is going to give you a standing ovation for real love. You certainly won’t get any help from the culture either. The world we live in has a distorted view of love. It will tell you that love is about gooey emotions, sex, fun, and hanging out with people you like. But real love is work. Moses is going to show you how love works in real life, and it’s by showing people patience. This week, Moses is going to have to learn to be patient with the Israelites. Most people don’t know how love works, but this week you are going to find out a few secrets most guys your age won’t learn for years. Let’s jump in.

days.title: Love Is Patient
days.scripture: COLOSSIANS 3:12-15 Since God chose you to be the holy people he loves, you must clothe yourselves with tenderhearted mercy, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience. Make allowance for each other’s faults, and forgive anyone who offends you. Remember, the Lord forgave you, so you must forgive others. Above all, clothe yourselves with love, which binds us all together in perfect harmony. And let the peace that comes from Christ rule in your hearts. For as members of one body you are called to live in peace. And always be thankful.
days.reflection: The idea of love is great. The reality of love is hard. Why? People. People can drive you crazy. Your parents can drive you crazy; your siblings can drive you crazy; your friends can drive you crazy. Loving people is hard, and loving people over the long haul is even harder. But you know what’s harder than love? Loneliness. Selfishness. Immaturity. If you are going to shift from being a selfish boy to a loving man, you have to be honest about how difficult it is to show patience to others. In 1 Corinthians 13, Paul says a few tiny words about how love works itself out in relationships. They are short but powerful. He says “Love is patient.” This week, you are going to watch Moses have to learn patience with the Israelites. You see, it’s easy to love people when things are going well. It’s easy to love people when God is doing miracles, the Egyptians are handing you silver and gold, and you are being released from 400 years of slavery. You don’t need patience when momentum is high and everyone is feeling good. But life never stays like that. What then? What do you do when things get hard? Moses is going to learn that the Israelites have been really beaten up as slaves. They are insecure, volatile, and afraid. Moses is going to have to love them through their ups and down, complaints and cries, fear and failure. He’s going to learn that it’s much harder to lead people into their future than to freedom. The truth is, without God's help, you are not going to be able to love God or others properly. You can’t whip love up and rely on your feelings. You will need God to put his love in your heart so you can pour it out to others. But the good news is, he will do exactly that. Romans 5:5 says, “For we know how dearly God loves us, because he has given us the Holy Spirit to fill our hearts with his love.” We don’t have to rely on our own patience or our own feelings; if you wait on God and seek him sincerely, he will fill your heart with love. And the love that God shows us spills over into others. 1 John 4:19-21 says, “We love each other because he loved us first. If someone says, “I love God,” but hates a fellow believer, that person is a liar; for if we don’t love people we can see, how can we love God, whom we cannot see? And he has given us this command: Those who love God must also love their fellow believers.” Although being patient with people is hard, it's worth it. Think about your own life. Aren’t you glad your parents have been patient with you? Aren’t you glad your teachers are patient with you? And aren’t you glad that God is patient with you? Love is patient. Moses will learn this, and this week, you will too.
questions: Who is the most patient person you know with other people? How do they show it?
On a scale of 1-10, how patient do you think you are with others? Can you share why you gave this answer?
Being honest and vulnerable, who do you struggle being patient with? Why? Ask the person you are doing this with who or what that is for him.

thoughts.verse: The LORD is compassionate and merciful, slow to get angry and filled with unfailing love… He does not punish us for all our sins; he does not deal harshly with us, as we deserve. For his unfailing love toward those who fear him is as great as the height of the heavens above the earth.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you patience with people who drive you crazy. Take some time to thank God for the ways he has been patient with you.

days.title: Patience When People Freak You Out
days.scripture: EXODUS 15:22-27, 17:1-7 Exodus 15 Then Moses led Israel from the Red Sea and they went into the Desert of Shur. For three days they traveled in the desert without finding water. When they came to Marah, they could not drink its water because it was bitter. (That is why the place is called Marah. ) So the people grumbled against Moses, saying, “What are we to drink?” Then Moses cried out to the LORD, and the LORD showed him a piece of wood. He threw it into the water, and the water became fit to drink. There the LORD issued a ruling and instruction for them and put them to the test. He said, “If you listen carefully to the LORD your God and do what is right in his eyes, if you pay attention to his commands and keep all his decrees, I will not bring on you any of the diseases I brought on the Egyptians, for I am the LORD, who heals you.” Then they came to Elim, where there were twelve springs and seventy palm trees, and they camped there near the water. Exodus 17 At the LORD’s command, the whole community of Israel left the wilderness of Sin and moved from place to place. Eventually they camped at Rephidim, but there was no water there for the people to drink. So once more the people complained against Moses. “Give us water to drink!” they demanded. “Quiet!” Moses replied. “Why are you complaining against me? And why are you testing the LORD?” But tormented by thirst, they continued to argue with Moses. “Why did you bring us out of Egypt? Are you trying to kill us, our children, and our livestock with thirst?” Then Moses cried out to the LORD, “What should I do with these people? They are ready to stone me!” The LORD said to Moses, “Walk out in front of the people. Take your staff, the one you used when you struck the water of the Nile, and call some of the elders of Israel to join you. I will stand before you on the rock at Mount Sinai. Strike the rock, and water will come gushing out. Then the people will be able to drink.” So Moses struck the rock as he was told, and water gushed out as the elders looked on. Moses named the place Massah (which means “test”) and Meribah (which means “arguing”) because the people of Israel argued with Moses and tested the LORD by saying, “Is the LORD here with us or not?”
days.reflection: Do you remember your first day of middle school? How did you feel? Confident, happy, full of peace? Probably not. When most guys enter a new situation, they tend to feel intimated, overwhelmed, and stressed out. As humans, we tend to crave comfort. We even crave comfort when things are bad for us. We prefer what we know, even if it makes us miserable. Facing change and going into new environments can be super hard. Understanding that people tend to freak out in new situations is key to being patient with people. The children of Israel have been slaves for 400 years. They have had to work like crazy building a tyrant’s kingdom. Now they are free but they will struggle to get that slavery mentality out of their heads. Even though they hated their lives, they had a constant source of food and shelter. They were treated horribly, but they had plenty of food. Now they have no idea where their food or water is going to come from and they panic. They are going to have to learn to trust God and not Pharaoh to provide for them. But it takes time. They don’t do well at first. 24 So the people grumbled against Moses, saying, “What are we to drink?” At the LORD’s command, the whole community of Israel left the wilderness of Sin and moved from place to place. Eventually they camped at Rephidim, but there was no water there for the people to drink. So once more the people complained against Moses. “Give us water to drink!” they demanded. “Quiet!” Moses replied. “Why are you complaining against me? And why are you testing the LORD?” But tormented by thirst, they continued to argue with Moses. “Why did you bring us out of Egypt? Are you trying to kill us, our children, and our livestock with thirst?” Grumbling and complaining, freaked out and fearful. This is what people do when they feel threatened. It’s probably what you do too. It’s what most of us tend to do. The Israelites will have to learn to trust God. The same God that delivered them will provide for them, but that’s easier said than done when you feel like you are dying of thirst. So how can we be patient when people are freaking out? 1- Go to God. Look at how Moses responds: 4 Then Moses cried out to the LORD, “What should I do with these people? They are ready to stone me!” He didn’t know how to handle the situation, so instead of just freaking out, he prays and asks God for help. He looks beyond himself to the one who has the power to solve the problem. Getting some distance from the frustration is important. 2- He obeys what God tells him. “Walk out in front of the people. Take your staff, the one you used when you struck the water of the Nile, and call some of the elders of Israel to join you. I will stand before you on the rock at Mount Sinai. Strike the rock, and water will come gushing out. Then the people will be able to drink.” So Moses struck the rock as he was told, and water gushed out as the elders looked on. God wanted the same staff that struck the Nile to strike the rock. Remember how the Egyptians saw the Nile River as their source of life? Now God wants the children of Israel to remember that he is the source of life. He has the power to provide water when they need it. It would be amazing if people went through life happy, trusting God, and never freaking out. But that’s not how people act. If you are going to be a man who lives for others, you are going to have to put up with some grumbling. You are going to have to show patience with others. People freak out under stress. God will help you not to. Be patient, keep calm, and carry on.
questions: Can you name a time when you faced a new situation and freaked out?
Is there an area of your life where you feel stuck doing something you know isn’t good, but you have gotten comfortable with it and don’t know how to change?
If you were Moses and could say anything to the children of Israel at this moment, what would it be?

thoughts.verse: Always be humble and gentle. Be patient with each other, making allowance for each other’s faults because of your love.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you patience with people who feel overwhelmed and insecure. Ask God to give you grace for areas you may be worried about and struggling to trust him in right now.

days.title: Patience For The Ungrateful
days.scripture: EXODUS 16:1-36 The whole Israelite community set out from Elim and came to the Desert of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they had come out of Egypt. In the desert the whole community grumbled against Moses and Aaron. The Israelites said to them, “If only we had died by the LORD’s hand in Egypt! There we sat around pots of meat and ate all the food we wanted, but you have brought us out into this desert to starve this entire assembly to death.” Then the LORD said to Moses, “I will rain down bread from heaven for you. The people are to go out each day and gather enough for that day. In this way I will test them and see whether they will follow my instructions. On the sixth day they are to prepare what they bring in, and that is to be twice as much as they gather on the other days.” So Moses and Aaron said to all the Israelites, “In the evening you will know that it was the LORD who brought you out of Egypt, and in the morning you will see the glory of the LORD, because he has heard your grumbling against him. Who are we, that you should grumble against us?” Moses also said, “You will know that it was the LORD when he gives you meat to eat in the evening and all the bread you want in the morning, because he has heard your grumbling against him. Who are we? You are not grumbling against us, but against the LORD.” Then Moses told Aaron, “Say to the entire Israelite community, ‘Come before the LORD, for he has heard your grumbling.’” While Aaron was speaking to the whole Israelite community, they looked toward the desert, and there was the glory of the LORD appearing in the cloud. The LORD said to Moses, “I have heard the grumbling of the Israelites. Tell them, ‘At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will be filled with bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God.’” That evening quail came and covered the camp, and in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp. When the dew was gone, thin flakes like frost on the ground appeared on the desert floor. When the Israelites saw it, they said to each other, “What is it?” For they did not know what it was. Moses said to them, “It is the bread the LORD has given you to eat. This is what the LORD has commanded: ‘Everyone is to gather as much as they need. Take an omer for each person you have in your tent.’” The Israelites did as they were told; some gathered much, some little. And when they measured it by the omer, the one who gathered much did not have too much, and the one who gathered little did not have too little. Everyone had gathered just as much as they needed. Then Moses said to them, “No one is to keep any of it until morning.” However, some of them paid no attention to Moses; they kept part of it until morning, but it was full of maggots and began to smell. So Moses was angry with them. Each morning everyone gathered as much as they needed, and when the sun grew hot, it melted away. On the sixth day, they gathered twice as much—two omers for each person—and the leaders of the community came and reported this to Moses. He said to them, “This is what the LORD commanded: ‘Tomorrow is to be a day of sabbath rest, a holy sabbath to the LORD. So bake what you want to bake and boil what you want to boil. Save whatever is left and keep it until morning.’” So they saved it until morning, as Moses commanded, and it did not stink or get maggots in it. “Eat it today,” Moses said, “because today is a sabbath to the LORD. You will not find any of it on the ground today. 26 Six days you are to gather it, but on the seventh day, the Sabbath, there will not be any.” Nevertheless, some of the people went out on the seventh day to gather it, but they found none. Then the LORD said to Moses, “How long will you refuse to keep my commands and my instructions? Bear in mind that the LORD has given you the Sabbath; that is why on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. Everyone is to stay where they are on the seventh day; no one is to go out.” So the people rested on the seventh day. The people of Israel called the bread manna. It was white like coriander seed and tasted like wafers made with honey. Moses said, “This is what the LORD has commanded: ‘Take an omer of manna and keep it for the generations to come, so they can see the bread I gave you to eat in the wilderness when I brought you out of Egypt.’” So Moses said to Aaron, “Take a jar and put an omer of manna in it. Then place it before the LORD to be kept for the generations to come.” As the LORD commanded Moses, Aaron put the manna with the tablets of the covenant law, so that it might be preserved. The Israelites ate manna forty years, until they came to a land that was settled; they ate manna until they reached the border of Canaan.
days.reflection: Most people are ungrateful. It’s a fact of life. We are so used to getting what we want that we melt down the second we don’t. We have to be patient with ungrateful people and love them anyway. It’s distinctive of our faith. In one of Jesus’ most radical teachings, he says, “Love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back. Then your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and wicked. Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.” (Luke 6:35- 36) God is kind to the ungrateful and wicked. You have to learn to be too. I know this sounds crazy, and it's super hard to do, but it’s what makes us Christians. It’s a part of what makes us men. God is kind to you when you are ungrateful. God is kind to you when you take him for granted. God is kind to you when you sin. You have to pass that on to others. You have to learn to show others the mercy you have been shown when you have been ungrateful. You see immature people often lack perspective. They have short-term thinking and short-term memories. That’s partly why they are ungrateful. The Israelites have been free for about 6 weeks, and they are starting to forget all that God did for them. Their memories are already being distorted. They remember the meat they ate in Egypt but forget their slavery. So, they complain about what they used to have to Moses. “If only we had died by the LORD’s hand in Egypt! There we sat around pots of meat and ate all the food we wanted, but you have brought us out into this desert to starve this entire assembly to death.” So what does God do? He doesn’t send down fire from heaven and destroy them. He doesn’t launch the plagues on them. He provides for them. He has patience as they are learning to live as free people. He shows the children of Israel mercy. When Moses cries out to God, he answers and gives them what they need. 11 The LORD said to Moses, 12 “I have heard the grumbling of the Israelites. Tell them, ‘At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will be filled with bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God.’” But they had to learn something different. Under Pharaoh, they had to earn their food; under Yahweh, it will be a gift. That’s why God instituted the Sabbath. He wanted them to have a day of rest where they learned to trust in him. God wanted them to know they didn’t have to earn their food. Again, we see this was hard for them. Some of them went out and tried to work on the Sabbath, but the food went moldy. It's hard to learn to trust when things are new; it’s hard to be grateful when the pressure is on. It’s hard to lead people who are stressed out. But situations like this show us that we can rely on God's mercy, even when people are ungrateful. Moses did, and you can too.
questions: Has someone ever been ungrateful for something you have done for them? What was it, and how did it make you feel?
Who is one of the most grateful people you know? How do they show it?
Take a moment and think of 2 or 3 things you are grateful for right now. What are they? Why do they matter to you?

thoughts.verse: Let all that I am praise the LORD; may I never forget the good things he does for me. He forgives all my sins and heals all my diseases. He redeems me from death and crowns me with love and tender mercies. He fills my life with good things. My youth is renewed like the eagle’s!
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his patience with you. Offer a few words of thanks and gratitude for the things you mentioned.

days.title: Patience In Prayer
days.scripture: EXODUS 17:8-16 The Amalekites came and attacked the Israelites at Rephidim. Moses said to Joshua, “Choose some of our men and go out to fight the Amalekites. Tomorrow I will stand on top of the hill with the staff of God in my hands.” So Joshua fought the Amalekites as Moses had ordered, and Moses, Aaron and Hur went to the top of the hill. As long as Moses held up his hands, the Israelites were winning, but whenever he lowered his hands, the Amalekites were winning. When Moses’ hands grew tired, they took a stone and put it under him and he sat on it. Aaron and Hur held his hands up—one on one side, one on the other—so that his hands remained steady till sunset. So Joshua overcame the Amalekite army with the sword. Then the LORD said to Moses, “Write this on a scroll as something to be remembered and make sure that Joshua hears it, because I will completely blot out the name of Amalek from under heaven.” Moses built an altar and called it The LORD is my Banner. He said, “Because hands were lifted up against the throne of the LORD, the LORD will be at war against the Amalekites from generation to generation.”
days.reflection: If you have ever played a video game, you know they always start out easy. You feel like you are doing well and it’s not that hard. Then boom - challenges start to hit you one by one. Things get more complex, and, before you know it, you are having to face 10 things at once. It gets harder and harder. You have to learn to face more than one challenge at a time to move forward. Life is actually a lot like that. Things start off nice and easy, then, before you know it, you are dealing with a hundred things at once. You really start feeling that pressure around your age. The teenage years can be overwhelming. Homework, temptation, pressure from friends, social media, exams, trying to fit in, struggling to learn. No wonder so many teenagers get stressed out. Teenagers need friends and leaders who will pray for them, encourage them, and lift them up in the midst of everything going on. The children of Israel have just been freed from Egypt and are learning to really trust Yahweh. They are trying to restart their lives when another crisis arises. They are attacked by the Amalekites. They too are having to learn to face multiple problems at once. The Amalekites are bullies. They preyed on the Israelites when they were weak and vulnerable. Deuteronomy 25:17-18 gives us a few more details about them: “Never forget what the Amalekites did to you as you came from Egypt. They attacked you when you were exhausted and weary, and they struck down those who were straggling behind. They had no fear of God.” The Amalekites hated God's people, Hundreds of years later, in the book of Esther, Haman will try and wipe out God's people; He was a descendant of the Amalekites. What does Moses do? He gets above the battle and lifts his hands to God in prayer. He knows that he doesn’t have the power to fight the Amalekites alone. He needs God to get involved and fight on their behalf. And that’s what God does. When Moses’ hands are raised, the Israelites surge. When his hands go down, they struggle. This is a key lesson to learn about loving others. You are going to deal with major challenges as you grow. The people you love and care for will face challenges too big for you to handle on your own. You are going to need to learn to get above the fight and ask God for help. Men who live for others pray for others. Men who live for others need God's help to fight the battles for the people they care about. Jesus often withdrew to pray. Moses went above the fight to seek God. Joshua sought God before his battles. David sought God in the middle of his challenges. Paul sought God in the middle of spiritual battles. God is calling you to learn to be a man of prayer, a man who lifts up others before God. He wants you to learn to rely on him in your leadership, and seek him when things get hard. You may not see yourself as a “man of prayer” right now, but God is training you day by day. He will continue to grow your relationship and confidence in him. You are well on your way. Get above the challenges you are facing. He loves to hear prayers from young men like you.
questions: What is the biggest challenge you are facing right now? What are some of the things that make you feel stressed out?
Do you find praying for others hard or easy? Why? It's okay to be totally honest.
Ask the person you are doing this study with about how they learned to pray for others. What do they find rewarding and what do they find hard about prayer?

thoughts.verse: Don’t worry about anything; instead, pray about everything. Tell God what you need, and thank him for all he has done. Then you will experience God’s peace, which exceeds anything we can understand. His peace will guard your hearts and minds as you live in Christ Jesus.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for help in any areas that are stressing you out. Ask him to help make you a man of prayer who relies on him.

days.title: Sustaining A Life For Others
days.scripture: EXODUS 18:1-27 Now Jethro, the priest of Midian and father-in-law of Moses, heard of everything God had done for Moses and for his people Israel, and how the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt. After Moses had sent away his wife Zipporah, his father-in-law Jethro received her and her two sons. One son was named Gershom, for Moses said, “I have become a foreigner in a foreign land”; and the other was named Eliezer, for he said, “My father’s God was my helper; he saved me from the sword of Pharaoh.” Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, together with Moses’ sons and wife, came to him in the wilderness, where he was camped near the mountain of God. Jethro had sent word to him, “I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you with your wife and her two sons.” So Moses went out to meet his father-in-law and bowed down and kissed him. They greeted each other and then went into the tent. Moses told his father-in-law about everything the LORD had done to Pharaoh and the Egyptians for Israel’s sake and about all the hardships they had met along the way and how the LORD had saved them. Jethro was delighted to hear about all the good things the LORD had done for Israel in rescuing them from the hand of the Egyptians. He said, “Praise be to the LORD, who rescued you from the hand of the Egyptians and of Pharaoh, and who rescued the people from the hand of the Egyptians. Now I know that the LORD is greater than all other gods, for he did this to those who had treated Israel arrogantly.” Then Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, brought a burnt offering and other sacrifices to God, and Aaron came with all the elders of Israel to eat a meal with Moses’ father-in-law in the presence of God. The next day Moses took his seat to serve as judge for the people, and they stood around him from morning till evening. When his father-in-law saw all that Moses was doing for the people, he said, “What is this you are doing for the people? Why do you alone sit as judge, while all these people stand around you from morning till evening?” returned and covered all the chariots and charioteers—the entire army of Pharaoh. Of all the Egyptians who had chased the Israelites into the sea, not a single one survived. But the people of Israel had walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground, as the water stood up like a wall on both sides. That is how the LORD rescued Israel from the hand of the Egyptians that day. And the Israelites saw the bodies of the Egyptians washed up on the seashore. When the people of Israel saw the mighty power that the LORD had unleashed against the Egyptians, they were filled with awe before him. They put their faith in the LORD and in his servant Moses. Moses answered him, “Because the people come to me to seek God’s will. Whenever they have a dispute, it is brought to me, and I decide between the parties and inform them of God’s decrees and instructions.” Moses’ father-in-law replied, “What you are doing is not good. You and these people who come to you will only wear yourselves out. The work is too heavy for you; you cannot handle it alone. Listen now to me and I will give you some advice, and may God be with you. You must be the people’s representative before God and bring their disputes to him. Teach them his decrees and instructions, and show them the way they are to live and how they are to behave. But select capable men from all the people—men who fear God, trustworthy men who hate dishonest gain —and appoint them as officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties and tens. Have them serve as judges for the people at all times, but have them bring every difficult case to you; the simple cases they can decide themselves. That will make your load lighter, because they will share it with you. If you do this and God so commands, you will be able to stand the strain, and all these people will go home satisfied.” Moses listened to his father-in-law and did everything he said. He chose capable men from all Israel and made them leaders of the people, officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties and tens. They served as judges for the people at all times. The difficult cases they brought to Moses, but the simple ones they decided themselves. Then Moses sent his father-in-law on his way, and Jethro returned to his own country.
days.reflection: Living to serve others is hard. It can be exhausting; it can be overwhelming. It can feel like it’s just too much. And it can be if you try to do everything on your own. You have to fight selfishness, but you don’t want to be stupid. Moses is going to learn a powerful lesson from his father-in-law today about how to live for others in a strategic and sustainable way. Moses has a problem. There were too many people for him to care for directly and too many problems for him to solve. When his father-in-law showed up, he couldn’t believe all that Moses was trying to do. Moses had people lining up day and night to get his advice, hear God's opinion, and solve fights. Jethro points out 2 things to Moses about how he is working. Moses’ workload was too much “What you are doing is not good. You and these people who come to you will only wear yourselves out. The work is too heavy for you; you cannot handle it alone. Moses has limits. All of us do. You need to be honest about what you are capable of and can handle in a given season. None of us are superhuman, and that’s ok. You need to learn to say “enough” when you are maxed out. Moses needed help “But select capable men from all the people—men who fear God, trustworthy men who hate dishonest gain —and appoint them as officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties and tens. Have them serve as judges for the people at all times, but have them bring every difficult case to you; the simple cases they can decide themselves. That will make your load lighter, because they will share it with you. If you do this and God so commands, you will be able to stand the strain, and all these people will go home satisfied.” You need a team of folks to really serve others well. A high-performing team will always beat a hard-working individual. Find what you can do, and leave the rest to others you trust. God doesn’t want you to kill yourself helping others. What good would that be? He wants you to serve others in a strategic and sustainable way. Your capacity will change over time, and you will want feedback from wiser folks in your life like Jethro was to Moses. This week you have learned a ton of stuff about living for others. You have learned that living for others requires patience. You have learned to be patient when others complain. You have learned to be patient when others are ungrateful. You have learned to be smart when you feel overwhelmed by others’ needs. Seriously, this is huge. You are working on stuff guys your age barely even think about. You are getting a huge advantage in growing into a strong and wise man, so hold your head high, and keep pressing in.
questions: Can you think of a time when you were totally overwhelmed and felt like you couldn’t do anything else? What was going on? How did it affect you?
Who do you think handles stress well? Why?
What do you find to be the most exhausting thing about serving others?

thoughts.verse: All praise to God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. God is our merciful Father and the source of all comfort. He comforts us in all our troubles so that we can comfort others. When they are troubled, we will be able to give them the same comfort God has given us.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you wisdom on how to love and serve others. Ask him for insight on how to serve without stressing out.

extras: WEEK 7 MANSCHOOL DRIVING ME CRAZY MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: DRIVING ME CRAZY Most people have zero patience. We hate having to wait for others. But the only way you learn is by being in frustrating situations. Go out for dinner, but try go when you know there will be rush hour traffic. See which lane is going more slowly, then pick that one. Then stop by the store and try and find the longest line, choose that one. Then let people in front of you at the restaurant. While you are having dinner, observe what happened when you had to wait. Discuss the importance of patience in a man's life. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch Finding Forrester A great story about an older recuse writer investing in a younger leader. A great example of the power of patience with people. And the classic line, "You're the man now, dog!" Trailer: https://youtu.be/8QGCBH_2Dck Or Watch I’ll Push You If you want an inspiring movie about 2 men walking the Camino De Santiago and maybe sowing some fuel for a journey yourself, check out this epic film about moving from self to others. Trailer: https://youtu.be/W7gKD3q0-V0 OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son to his favorite restaurant and recap the week. This week contains some really powerful life lessons. Very few guys your
